speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying, Mr. Eizenstein, that you spent-- you were in Berlin. And do you want to take it back-- we want to take it back a bit to when the Polish army was being organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] What can I say, you know? So much to say, and I don't know what to say. They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we finish-- when we finished in Berlin, they took our unit to Katowice really to prepare ourselves to send our unit to Japan. Until we got ready, they threw the bomb, so they cancel us. They canceled the whole thing. And then the Polish terrorists, they come from Britain. They call themself [INAUDIBLE]. They were killing us who wasn't Polish uniform or Russian uniform. They poisoned us. They kill us. Or they gave us orders in civil clothes to send us to families. They made the pogrom in Kielce. Then they made a pogram in-- they want to make up a pogrom in [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't succeed, thank to me. I don't want to go into it, but if you wanted proof, someone can ask me, I can prove it. I stopped it. The pogrom in-- in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put us in civil clothes and then sent us to private homes. And they're paying 17 dollar-- 17 ruble a month, board and room. We went in civil clothes. And I have to be discharged in 1940-- 1945 and they discharged me, they kept me uniform-- not me, lots of us-- they kept us till 1946. And they-- they give me-- and they give me a villa in [? Gleisdorf ?]. I get a big-- from the Germans, I can mention a name. It was a general, very big general. They gave it to me, what you call them? A ranch. Hunting six-- 60-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. Six acre. Yeah, six acre hunting area, fenced, and land in a valley. And German prisoners were working in the valley. Why? I did know nothing about farming. And I was in the army, and this they give me. For the weekend I used to come and spend a good time, you know, parties-- girls, boys, you know. And I kept it for a year, that. Anyways-- And you were still technically in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Technically in the army on account they find out the Americans and the British and the French made a deal with the Germans to organize and invade Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was very secret, so the top guys, they kept it in. You know, they kept us without uniform, and they give us, you know, the cream. And finally they got me, they poisoned-- eight guys of us. The [INAUDIBLE] made us a party, and they poison us. Me and my buddy survived on account we put in two fingers. I knew survival. Two fingers we cut it. Maybe we didn't get the real, you know, so much poison, but the rest, eight guys on the table, passed out. And even the floor-- later when we come in from the hospital, the floor, when we give it up, and the floor was all-- from our stomach, me and [? Fadia ?]. I got a picture with him. And since then I was suffering, and I didn't trust nobody. So I give up everything, and I find out from-- in Wroclaw, this big concentration Jewish. Jewish from Russia who survived, from Poland, and from there descended to Italy, from Italy over to Germany, from there they go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to find out what had happened to your family, your relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the [INAUDIBLE]. When we were in Lublin, they brought us fresh soldiers. Lots of us got hit. And a guy come in, and he says to me, if you could dial your son, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, and they call nickname, [INAUDIBLE]. My grandfather used to have a business, you know, and the village they called him [INAUDIBLE]. You know, they didn't have teeth. You know, the Jewish way, [INAUDIBLE], you know, Polish and Russian, they didn't know. [INAUDIBLE] says [INAUDIBLE], so that's what everybody in that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you come in and you ask for [INAUDIBLE] or Ajzenstein or [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE], you had everybody got a nickname. That's the only way. Even myself, I didn't know the names, just nicknames. You understand? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he come and he told me what happened to our town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My leader was a Cossack. My boss from my unit, he wanted organized and go, was but 90-- [? three ?] days' drive to go there in my town to get revenge.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when we moved out, we met in-- they call them [INAUDIBLE], Ukrainian rightist, who they were working with the fascist. They went in to have their own partisans [? write ?] the Russians, want to get him. You understand what I'm talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went in the bushes, all the-- the cooperators with the Nazis, and they're very powerful. So they told us, the intelligent, not go back. You know, why they did not-- we're not going to make it. They're going destroy us. They are powerful, particular in our district.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back home to our unit after two or three days traveling. We got lots opposition but we destroyed the villages, we burn it. I'll be honest with you, we burned the villages to the ground. They didn't let up nobody there. Is that how you found out that your mother did not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we find out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why I didn't believe, you know, what the Germans could do, the Ukrainian what they did. And then they told me the Ukrainian shot my mom. She went for water to the pump, the Ukrainian shot her. For what? For what reason?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I become animal. Since then, I'll be honest with me, I call myself animal. I'm not responsible what I did, and I don't know even what I did. So after the war they took us-- a few guys, they took us to clinic, I don't remember, in Poland. And they kept us for two weeks, and they told us we are normal. We can go back to the service.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was no humane. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was killing dogs no matter what, what is in my way. Anything I could get, what moved, we did it. And the best thing was they give us 24 hours' freedom and [? you see ?] that we took it. They give us 24 hours' freedom. I don't know what I did. And the biggest fight was when I was involved battle from street to street, from room to room, from apartment to apartment, was Friedland. There's old [? Friedland ?] and then you got the water, a river, and the New [? Friedland. ?] When we got into [? New Friedland, ?] three times we got in to capture the city, three times we have to leave. Thousands of us got killed. They were throwing stones, hard water, tar, hard tar. So the third time we went, we fight, no missionary. They give us 24 hours. [INAUDIBLE] himself and all marshals tell us 48 hours they give us freedom, not 24 hours, if we captured the city. I don't know what was it with me. I survived. But what the Germans did, when we find out what they did to people and the partisan come to told us and what they did to the people, what they took the kids, pregnant woman, the Ukrainian militia and the Germans militia. They took kids, and they cut the veins in the pails, the plastic pails, blood. Kids, babies. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Is not to describe what these animals did, particular was Hungarian, all Slavish, you know, units, Gestapo. They are worse like the Germans. In our town, he told me-- [INAUDIBLE] told me in our town was just eight Germans. Every day they ask for six virgin girls-- I'm sorry, eight virgin girls, so many minx coats, and so much gold delivered. When everything is finished, no more virgins, no more gold, then they bury them. They dig their own holes. One survivor ran away is my uncle, my mother's brother. He told me the whole story. He died in Israel. I got him off in a [INAUDIBLE]. Then, you know, I mix it up. I wanted everything to be-- and another thing. Stalin give orders after war in Berlin. We got in flyers, each one-- not flyers. They give us letters, I don't know. If you're looking for family to write to Stalin. Really, Stalin doesn't, you know but--

speaker: interviewer
text: His office.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's the name was to write to Stalin. So what I want to say in Katowice, they brought me my mother's brother, [? Benny ?] Girszlak with a little machine, [INAUDIBLE] machine. He used to make caps. Two KGB guys to sign it. [INAUDIBLE] in Katowice, you know, the street what I lived, what the government paid for me and civil clothes on account the Polish terrorist, you know, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: We liberate Poland-- Polish. They come from Britain to send in bandits to kill us to take over the government. Where is the justice?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so they brought in-- I just want to say what they said, blame Stalin for everything. All this government, 80% were Jews. Marshals, generals were Jews. And another thing I forgot to tell you, the most important thing about Stalin. I was in the front line, and they took us away with airplanes to Poland for Jewish Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah to the rabbis in [INAUDIBLE]. And after the war, we're in Katowice, they took me too to [INAUDIBLE] to a rabbi for two weeks. I didn't know when was Rosh Hashanah. But it was written, [INAUDIBLE] Rosh Hashanah, Jewish nationality, know, Moses' religion, in order to register. And they paid for it, and they gave us stickers and everything to go to rabbi for Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if they did that in American army or the British army or the French army. They call them the Allies, the nice guys. They did it and Stalin did it, Polish government did it. Why they blame them? Now, all the survivors, millions are survived from Stalin. We're not-- the same rights. They're all survivors from Russia. Who-- who took them in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, a brother for my father survived. He was a Polish prisoner, was a lieutenant in Krakow, in the queen's brigade, horse-- horse brigade. He was the only Jew, a lieutenant. He used to come to us for Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah. The two-- the two guards run to Polish his-- and one a bodyguard, one to cook for him. I don't know what--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was before the war? The uncle--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in time from Poland, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war. Now, you mentioned that your mother's brother, Benzim Girszlak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they brought him to me in Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they brought him in person to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and he was 25 years in jail for a black marketeer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Years, yeah. After he got out from the-- from the partisans, he went to Korets, and he was in the black market. So they give him 25 years. They send him to [INAUDIBLE]. But when I wrote to Stalin when looking for my family, I took Girszlak for my mother's side, Ajzenstein from my father's side. So they send me [INAUDIBLE]. They brought him to me in Katowice. I signed for him. I sent him right away to Germany, if I want to do the same thing there, you know, a black marketeer. Then to find my-- my father's brother, Meyer Ajzenstein. But they told me he commit so crime they can't let him up. He have to stay another few years. Anyways, they let him out. They send him to Vienna, and they send me a letter, the Russian console to pay if I want to get him to Canada. I didn't want to bring account of was very wounded. I sent a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was very wounded in the war. He was invalid. I cannot bring him with a wife and two kids. To whom? I didn't have myself where to stay. So I told him, I'll connect Israel. And Israel, State of Israel, I told them that's my uncle, I deserved it. So State of Israel sent him up ticket from Vienna to Israel. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in [? my ?] business. That's 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's already when you were in Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Canada. [? That was I said, ?] in Canada. And they give him a beautiful job. He got a telephone even and pension.

speaker: interviewer
text: So get--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I mean, how can you blame the-- the-- the Russians? What, there's conspiracy? The Americans are think they want to get rid of starting of Communism.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Communism is not so bad. I mean, people-- you take all the Jews, they come now from-- come from Stalin from-- everybody's educated. If in Canada and America, you don't go with the government, you break the law, you go in jail too. You understand? And I give you another example. When Stalin come into 1939, they took away gulags. Take my sister-in-law, [? Luba ?] [INAUDIBLE], right? She was a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: They took your family. They sent to Siberia, but they give them a home. In-- you know, in the old [INAUDIBLE], she's a professor. Your brother's a professor. Your sister's a professor in Russia for Stalin. And the mother survive. They all come to Israel, and they're all educated. And, I mean, what's so-- why you pick it up, why you destroy-- now, god forbid something-- something happened. We got no place to go, nor to protect us. And I know I don't trust America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting back--

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't mind a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's your tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. One general told me in Israel for UN. I was involved but Lebanese border was responsible there too, once. We become very close friend. And, to be honest with you, he married the rabbi's daughter. He become Jewish, even went to surgery. If you made a [? laugh, ?] when he come, we salute him. He was a general from Texas. Blond guy with blue eyes. I can't tell you his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told me one thing. He says to me, Borys, we Americans make a stink all over the world, but we never clean it up. We start it, but we never finish. And that's true. You take Vietnam. You take Korea. You take Lebanon, what they did, you know everywhere. What they did to Israel. They lock us up. They tie us up in [INAUDIBLE], beat you up. I mean, where's the democracy? And why the Jews-- You know, I would recommend the Jews smarten up, get your investment to Israel, send your kids to Israel. Why I come from Israel? I'll be honest, I couldn't take it. I was very sick. I can prove it to you, papers. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I couldn't take the heat. I was paralyzed my foot and here, and you can check the papers. Dr. [? Glass, ?] KK Glass.

speaker: interviewee
text: The police sent all information to him, and he took care of me in the clinic here. And he told me I can't go no place. I have to stay here in this weather. And now when I travel every year or every second year, I go to Israel. I always come back sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you don't believe me, ask Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. I went in two surgeries to that time to Israel, and I'm still going to Israel, and I can't-- in the lungs, I-- I-- I get sick there, you know? The weather doesn't agree with me. Let's go back to Bratislav, where you made some decisions.

speaker: interviewee
text: When when I-- I don't know. Be honest with you, not to tell you the [INAUDIBLE]. When I was in Katowice, I don't know [INAUDIBLE] made a Israeli writers' group find out about me, I don't know how. I don't know. Maybe Menzhinskii. I think he was connect with them. The [INAUDIBLE] Menzhinskii, somehow. They come to mine apartment and they told me I have to bring him up every day weapons, guns with bullets, eight bullets. So I risk my life every time I went to the general [INAUDIBLE], I was working there. I took up here, behind, event for dinner I took up a gun and eight bullets. That's what going on about six, seven months. And then what happened with [INAUDIBLE]. I did a job, beautiful job. I saved all the Jewish there. If you don't believe me, I can prove it. Till today, still there're survivors. And then they told me, you know, you have to go. I come into [INAUDIBLE], to Bratislav, and [? was ?] connected a landsman. And he was working for the KGB. He's a survivor, wasn't a partisan. But somehow his wife was Polish, and he was working for the KGB.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to his house to get information [? I ?] to go to Czechoslovakia-- I don't know where, to go [INAUDIBLE] to Palestine. We were sitting and having coffee, tea, or something-- food. And he-- he to me, you got ID? They're coming to get me, the Polish intelligent. After me. So he-- he threw me through the window somehow. I jumped. This was second or third story. Tied up, jumped, and I took off. And I start to look. I to get connection with the Jewish intelligent. So I went in a synagogue. I was alone, no money, no nothing. I left everything behind. You know, [INAUDIBLE] [? avenge-- ?] a job, a Jeep, everything, and I got nothing, nobody-- but, anyways, I find the landsman, [INAUDIBLE], in the [? convoy-- ?] you know the electric [? convoy ?] in the morning. You know, the electric train? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Electric train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Electric train is fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and the [INAUDIBLE]. He recognized me. You understand? And I recognize him. And he told me about my family. I got family. I got cousins, my auntie. And I went and I saw him, and they were already way to go to Germany. They didn't pay attention even to me. They didn't-- didn't bother them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only thing they told me, where you got your-- your [? medals. ?] Where you buy them? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's the [INAUDIBLE]. Anyways, I got connected with the [INAUDIBLE]. They took me in a place to fix me up, you know? They give me authority. They send me to do jobs, you know? I got myself organized with weapons, protection, good-- good organization with the guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did what we have to do, and was already too dangerous, so they send me to contact with Czechoslovakian generals, but they were Jewish. They brought me these-- [INAUDIBLE]. That's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: They [? threw me at the ?] Czechoslovakian border and there was two Jeeps with full uniform generals, Czechoslovakian army. They took me to the Jeep. They brought me to Prague, and they disappear. And from Prague, they took me to Alps to walk up, and we got into Vienna. You understand? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, I got in hospital. It's a whole story. In Vienna, I can tell you I got connected. Some guy told me in hospital, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm Austrian, and I told him, I'm Jewish. And he says to me, and I told him what you did to the Jews. And he says, I didn't do nothing. He was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said what you mean, he was a Jew? He says, I come from the town. I'll take you there. I can take you, all Jews. Hitler was [? made ?] by storekeeper, by a Jew. She was his-- what do you call them? Mistress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. She was housekeeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Housekeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and he says, he wasn't made by Jew and then when he grow up in this area, he want to go with Jewish girls. They told him [INAUDIBLE]. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not-- not here, not there. And he denied the father, you understand? And according Jewish law, if the mother's not Jewish, he's not a Jew. So he was [INAUDIBLE], so he took revenge. Anyways, that's what he told me. You believe it? Not, yes, not. I-- I don't know. But that's the way the old German guy-- and he took me to his country house. He showed me the [INAUDIBLE] there, and he kept me for three days like a king. I even got drunk with-- they got apple cider. It's so strong, he light it up. A match, it burns. And, you know, that's the way he told me. In [INAUDIBLE], they took me through the Alps to Milan. And in Milan, they took me into the partisans work for the-- for the Haganah. From the [INAUDIBLE]. Who knows? Was all different parties. But everybody got a different job, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they got me in the papers. I got black and white to prove. You saw it. And, I mean, I got the papers. For this [INAUDIBLE] Street and [INAUDIBLE] Street, I got the [INAUDIBLE]. For Israel, they couldn't get. And to the [INAUDIBLE] from Rome-- from Milan. And even after surgery, when I got-- the surgery was done to me, Professor Mazzero. He was one from the highest fascist professor. It was a private clinic. So they brought him from Israel, Haganah, to be with the surgery. A Jewish woman doctor from Milan in [INAUDIBLE] was mine surgery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of surgery did you require?

speaker: interviewee
text: What from the poison, what was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the poison in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So they have to put me, cut out the gastrica and the stomach. It was for-- and they couldn't give me-- I was so weak and so poisoned that they couldn't give me to sleep. They give me four injections like that. You know, like horses, you know? Four like liter bottles. Freeze me and he talk to me, alive, you know, and there was [? nuns. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a private clinic. He charged them 200 dollars American a day, Professor Mazzero, but Haganah paid for that. But who can get in to him? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So it's OK, that yeah? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you say you stayed in Italy for how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there from 1946 to 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll talk about your time in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From Italy is very important too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.
